title: "Dependencies, Dependency Injection, and Cross-Cutting Concerns in Modern Development"

categories: [architecture, dotnet, patterns]

toc_sticky: true

---


![Innject]({{ site.url }}{{ site.baseurl }}/assets/images/inject.png){: .align-center}

# Dependencies

Dependencies surround us everywhere. Only God created everything from nothing. People create things from other things. A builder needs materials, a gardener needs seeds, a writer needs ideas. Programmers also work with text — they express ideas and give them form through code. By some accounts, developers spend 90% of their time reading code written by others or by themselves. Text is the primary material, the very clay from which each of us tries, day after day, to sculpt a thought. For some, those thoughts come out clumsy; for others, they approach something close to perfection — though perfection, as we know, has no ceiling. Then other programmers can appreciate the beauty of a verbal masterpiece — or its caricature. The ideal. The almost ideal. Because the first and most fundamental dependency is the person themselves: their limited capabilities, their cognitive faculties, their imperfect character traits, and all the well-known human weaknesses.

Experience is a sequence of trials and errors. It is the memory of good and bad decisions, and the intuition — the gut feeling — that can itself be cultivated. When a programmer adds new functionality, they face a choice: reinvent the wheel, or stand on the shoulders of colleagues. The desire to create something new, unusual, and necessary is always there. It can seem as though all programs have already been written (non-programmers tend to believe this sincerely), that everything has been done, invented, discovered, and there is no room left for your ideas. But there is always someone who brings an idea to life and gives it a long future. That someone usually gets there before us. Someone smarter, more hardworking, and more persistent is always a step ahead.

We, ordinary programmers, depend on ideas and thoughts. On the ideas and thoughts of others. Reluctantly — very reluctantly — do programmers reach for someone else's solutions. The primary criterion, much as in other creative and craft disciplines, is beauty. If it looks beautiful, it is probably correct.

> *"If the code looks like shit — it probably works like shit."*
> — Unknown

But every new dependency, every new component, library, service, framework, class, or script added to a project increases its complexity. It makes you dependent on something, forces you to grapple with unfamiliar code and unfamiliar ideas. And yet the program needs to work. Work well. And have worked well the day before yesterday. (Sometimes, receiving a task, you are already late in delivering it.) It needs to look much better, too. And time? A constant dependency on time. And there's the need for cross-browser compatibility, cross-platform support, cross-temporal resilience, and whatever else comes next — an endless cross-country race toward the latest release of the best program, without a doubt.

Dependency is suspicious. Bugs live inside dependencies — worse, someone else's bugs. Dependencies demand attention, like demanding houseguests. You must learn to choose them wisely, spend time on their upkeep, learn quickly as you study them. Choosing them is an art, and their influence on any project is hard to overstate.

On the other hand, good dependencies — and there are many of them — free us from routine work. There is no need to resolve already-solved problems. No need to revisit old bicycles, tractors, and bulldozers. You can and should focus on your own task. An aeronautical engineer designing a fighter jet does not begin by building a mining combine, inventing a rolling mill, or standing at a forge to extract the necessary aluminum. To build a plane — a complex machine — you need raw material. To build a program, you need foundational components, building blocks. A solid foundation yields a solid project. The most successful projects reuse up to 70% of borrowed code [Grady Booch]. Dependencies define the future project, and the correct introduction of those dependencies is simultaneously an art, a science, and a craft. We will explore dependencies, how they are introduced, and how they are chosen using the .NET platform as our example.

---

## Cities That Grow, and Cities That Were Designed

Before we talk about code — let's talk about cities. The analogy is not accidental.

Physicist Geoffrey West, in his book **_Scale_ (2017)** (which I recommend to everyone), discovered a scaling effect: data from cities around the world shows that infrastructure and energy consumption scale *sublinearly* — that is, they grow more slowly than the population (double a city's size, and infrastructure grows not 2x but 1.8x) — while indicators of social interaction scale *superlinearly*, faster than the population (double a city, and wages and innovation grow 2.2x). Simply put: roads and pipes grow slower than people, salaries and innovations grow faster. West uncovered universal laws of growth and self-organization that govern living systems, cities, and companies — and they all obey the same underlying principles. Cities are self-organizing networks, not engineered machines.

**Brasília** is the most famous counterexample. A capital city designed from scratch in 1956 by Le Corbusier's pupil Lúcio Costa and Oscar Niemeyer — on a blank plateau, free from the ballast of slums and colonial heritage. An architectural masterpiece, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. But the city was built entirely for the automobile. Its enormous spaces are not made for walking. Some argue that Brasília cannot be considered a real city because it lacks the necessary "anthropomorphic ingredients" — dirty streets, people walking to the office next door, spontaneous markets, chance encounters.

**What went wrong?** Something always goes wrong, doesn't it? People plan, and it usually doesn't turn out quite right. The city was designed top-down, shaped by the ideology of its architects rather than the needs of its inhabitants. A living city always grows from patterns of human behavior — bottom-up. This is precisely what West means when he speaks of self-organization: the complexity of urban life is not imposed by a master plan; it emerges from millions of small interactions.

This is a direct analogy for software development.

---

![Under construction — refactoring inevitable](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## UI as the Primary Dependency: Architecture from the Bottom Up

The traditional development approach: database first → business logic → UI. It sounds logical. In practice, it produces Brasília: a system designed top-down according to an ideal scheme — and when it finally reaches the user, living in it is uncomfortable, and everything has to be rewritten.

The user interface is not "a layer on top of" the architecture. **The UI is the assembly point of real requirements.** This is where the system meets the living user, where actual usage patterns reveal themselves — just as in an organic city, streets are laid where people walk, not where the architect drew a line.

Mark Seemann describes a classic anti-pattern: imagine an online shop where the user interface talks directly to database queries. If you migrate from SQL to NoSQL, you'll have to rewrite significant parts of the application. Applying DI principles, the UI communicates exclusively through well-defined interfaces — and when the database changes, it's enough to create a new implementation of the data access interface without touching the UI layer [Mark Seemann, *Dependency Injection in .NET*, Manning].

Notice: Seemann's own example is framed precisely from the UI perspective. Not "the database is changing" — but "the user works through a UI that demands flexibility." This distinction is fundamental.

### UI-First: What It Means in Practice

The traditional order (DB-first, Brasília):
```
Database → Repositories → Services → API → UI
```

The UI-first approach (organic city):
```
User Story → UI Mockup → API Contract → Services → Repositories → DB
```

This is not "draw the buttons first." It means that **the shape of data and the system's behavior are determined by the user's needs**, not by the convenience of storage.

```csharp
// ❌ DB-first: the data shape is dictated by the UI
public class ProductDto
{
    public int ProductId { get; set; }           // from table
    public int CategoryFk { get; set; }           // from DB schema
    public decimal UnitPriceWithTax { get; set; } // from stored procedure
}

// ✅ UI-first: the data shape reflects the user's need
public class ProductCardViewModel
{
    public string Name { get; set; }
    public string FormattedPrice { get; set; }   // "$12.99"
    public string CategoryLabel { get; set; }    // "Electronics"
    public bool IsAvailable { get; set; }
    public string[] Tags { get; set; }
}
```

This is precisely why DI is critical: it allows you to build the system in any direction without locking into specific implementations at the earliest stages.

---

## The Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)

![dependency inversion principle][image_ref_9s8wk88q]

**DIP (Dependency Inversion Principle)** — one of the five SOLID principles:

- High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on abstractions.
- Abstractions should not depend on details. Details should depend on abstractions.

> [Agile Principles, Practices and Patterns in C#]

```csharp
// ❌ DIP violation: high-level class depends on a concrete implementation
public class OrderService
{
    private readonly SqlOrderRepository _repository = new SqlOrderRepository();
}

// ✅ DIP compliance: dependency on an abstraction
public class OrderService
{
    private readonly IOrderRepository _repository;

    public OrderService(IOrderRepository repository)
    {
        _repository = repository;
    }
}
```

---

## Dependency Injection & IoC

![control inversion][image_ref_zhibd944]

> *"'Dependency Injection' is a 25-dollar term for a 5-cent concept. Dependency injection means giving an object its instance variables. Really. That's it."*
> — James Shore

**Dependency Injection** is a set of software design principles and patterns that enable the development of loosely coupled code.
> — Mark Seemann

**Inversion of Control (IoC)** is a pattern for inverting interfaces, control flow, and dependencies.
> — John Sonmez

**IoC Container** — a framework for dependency injection.

![Spaghetti dependencies without DI](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### Types of Dependencies

| Type | Examples |
|------|---------|
| **Explicit (public)** | Constructor parameters, properties |
| **Implicit (hidden)** | `new SomeService()`, static methods, singletons |
| **Internal** | Business logic, domain objects |
| **External** | Databases, file system, web services, third-party libraries |
| **Foundational** | Framework, runtime, OS |
| **Modern (2025)** | AI/LLM services, cloud providers, feature flags, language models |

---

## Dependency Injection Patterns

### 1. Constructor Injection (Recommended)

```csharp
public class PaymentService
{
    private readonly IPaymentGateway _gateway;
    private readonly ILogger<PaymentService> _logger;

    public PaymentService(IPaymentGateway gateway, ILogger<PaymentService> logger)
    {
        _gateway = gateway ?? throw new ArgumentNullException(nameof(gateway));
        _logger = logger;
    }

    public async Task<PaymentResult> ProcessAsync(PaymentRequest request)
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("Processing payment {Id}", request.Id);
        return await _gateway.ChargeAsync(request);
    }
}
```

### 2. Property Injection (Optional, with local defaults)

```csharp
public class ReportService
{
    // Optional dependency — works even without external injection
    public ILogger Logger { get; set; } = NullLogger.Instance;

    public void Generate(Report report)
    {
        Logger.LogInformation("Generating report: {Name}", report.Name);
    }
}
```

### 3. Method Injection (Dependency varies per call)

```csharp
public class DataProcessor
{
    // transformer varies depending on the call context
    public void Process(IDataSet dataSet, ITransformer transformer)
    {
        var result = transformer.Transform(dataSet);
    }
}
```

### 4. Factory Injection

```csharp
public class NotificationService
{
    private readonly Func<NotificationType, INotifier> _notifierFactory;

    public NotificationService(Func<NotificationType, INotifier> notifierFactory)
        => _notifierFactory = notifierFactory;

    public void Notify(NotificationType type, string message)
        => _notifierFactory(type).Send(message);
}
```

---

## Inversion of Control (IoC)

![inversions][image_ref_6dcv7r76]

Three kinds of inversion:

| Kind of Inversion | Description |
|---|---|
| **Interface Inversion** | Control over the interface between two components |
| **Flow Inversion** | Control over the execution flow (event-driven, Hollywood Principle) |
| **Creation Inversion** | Control over the creation and wiring of dependencies |

### IoC Container in .NET 8+

```csharp
// Program.cs
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>();
builder.Services.AddSingleton<ICache, MemoryCache>();
builder.Services.AddTransient<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();

// Modern dependencies
builder.Services.AddOpenAIClient(builder.Configuration["OpenAI:ApiKey"]);
builder.Services.AddAzureBlobStorage(builder.Configuration["Azure:Storage"]);
builder.Services.AddFeatureManagement();

var app = builder.Build();
```

### The Register → Resolve → Release Pattern

```
┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐
│  REGISTER   │───▶│   RESOLVE   │───▶│   RELEASE   │
│             │    │             │    │             │
│  Container  │    │  Obtain     │    │  Free       │
│  setup      │    │  dependencies│   │  resources  │
└─────────────┘    └─────────────┘    └─────────────┘
```

> **Hollywood Principle:** *"Don't call the container — it will call you."*

### Modern IoC Frameworks (.NET)

| IoC Framework | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|
| **Microsoft.Extensions.DI** | Built into .NET, official support | Minimalist API |
| **Autofac** | Flexible API, modules, decorators | Slightly steeper learning curve |
| **Scrutor** | Convention-based registration on top of MS DI | Depends on MS DI |
| **Castle Windsor** | Full-featured, decorator support, typed factories | Quirky API in places |
| **Ninject** | Lightweight, extensible | Slow performance |

> **2025:** For most .NET 8+ projects, the built-in container + `Scrutor` for convention-based registration is more than sufficient.

---

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

![cross cutting concerns][image_ref_3t79cktw]

Cross-cutting concerns are behaviors that affect many components but are not part of their core responsibility.

| Concern | Description |
|---|---|
| **Logging** | Recording events about application state |
| **Auditing** | Monitoring critical operations and data |
| **Caching** | Improving performance through cache |
| **Security** | Authorization and authentication |
| **Error Handling** | Catching and handling exceptions |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Circuit Breaker, Retry, Timeout |
| **Performance Counting** | Performance diagnostics |
| **Distributed Tracing** | OpenTelemetry — the modern successor to simple logging |

---

## AOP & Aspects

**Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP)** is a programming paradigm that allows global properties of a program to determine how it is compiled into an executable.

An **aspect** is a subroutine associated with a specific program property. When that property changes, the effect propagates throughout the entire program. An aspect is used as part of a new kind of compiler — the **aspect weaver**.

The core principles on which AOP is built:

- Wrapper / wrapping
- Single Responsibility Principle
- Decorator Pattern
- Open/Closed Principle

---

## AOP → Middleware: The Modern Approach

In 2025, most AOP tasks are addressed through the **Middleware Pipeline** (ASP.NET Core), the **Decorator Pattern**, **Behaviors** (MediatR), and **Interceptors**. The PostSharp approach with IL Weaving remains relevant for specialized scenarios.

### ASP.NET Core Middleware Pipeline

```csharp
public class RequestLoggingMiddleware
{
    private readonly RequestDelegate _next;
    private readonly ILogger<RequestLoggingMiddleware> _logger;

    public RequestLoggingMiddleware(RequestDelegate next, ILogger<RequestLoggingMiddleware> logger)
    {
        _next = next;
        _logger = logger;
    }

    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
    {
        var sw = Stopwatch.StartNew();
        _logger.LogInformation("→ {Method} {Path}", context.Request.Method, context.Request.Path);

        try
        {
            await _next(context);
        }
        finally
        {
            sw.Stop();
            _logger.LogInformation("← {Status} {Path} [{Elapsed}ms]",
                context.Response.StatusCode, context.Request.Path, sw.ElapsedMilliseconds);
        }
    }
}

app.UseMiddleware<RequestLoggingMiddleware>();
```

### Decorator — Universal AOP Without Magic

```csharp
public interface IProductRepository
{
    Task<Product?> GetByIdAsync(int id);
}

public class EfProductRepository : IProductRepository
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;
    public EfProductRepository(AppDbContext db) => _db = db;
    public Task<Product?> GetByIdAsync(int id) => _db.Products.FindAsync(id).AsTask();
}

// Caching decorator — AOP without PostSharp
public class CachedProductRepository : IProductRepository
{
    private readonly IProductRepository _inner;
    private readonly IMemoryCache _cache;

    public CachedProductRepository(IProductRepository inner, IMemoryCache cache)
    {
        _inner = inner;
        _cache = cache;
    }

    public Task<Product?> GetByIdAsync(int id)
        => _cache.GetOrCreateAsync($"product:{id}", _ => _inner.GetByIdAsync(id));
}

// Registration with Scrutor
services.AddScoped<IProductRepository, EfProductRepository>();
services.Decorate<IProductRepository, CachedProductRepository>();
```

### MediatR Pipeline Behaviors (CQRS + AOP)

```csharp
public class LoggingBehavior<TRequest, TResponse> : IPipelineBehavior<TRequest, TResponse>
    where TRequest : IRequest<TResponse>
{
    private readonly ILogger<LoggingBehavior<TRequest, TResponse>> _logger;

    public LoggingBehavior(ILogger<LoggingBehavior<TRequest, TResponse>> logger)
        => _logger = logger;

    public async Task<TResponse> Handle(
        TRequest request,
        RequestHandlerDelegate<TResponse> next,
        CancellationToken cancellationToken)
    {
        var name = typeof(TRequest).Name;
        _logger.LogInformation("[START] {Request}", name);
        var sw = Stopwatch.StartNew();

        var response = await next();

        _logger.LogInformation("[END] {Request} [{Elapsed}ms]", name, sw.ElapsedMilliseconds);
        return response;
    }
}

services.AddTransient(typeof(IPipelineBehavior<,>), typeof(LoggingBehavior<,>));
services.AddTransient(typeof(IPipelineBehavior<,>), typeof(ValidationBehavior<,>));
services.AddTransient(typeof(IPipelineBehavior<,>), typeof(CachingBehavior<,>));
```

---

## Modern Dependencies: AI, Cloud, LLM

By 2025, dependencies have expanded far beyond databases and third-party libraries.

### AI and Language Models as Ordinary Injections

```csharp
public interface IAIAssistant
{
    Task<string> CompleteAsync(string prompt, CancellationToken ct = default);
}

public class OpenAIAssistant : IAIAssistant
{
    private readonly OpenAIClient _client;
    private readonly string _model;

    public OpenAIAssistant(OpenAIClient client, IConfiguration config)
    {
        _client = client;
        _model = config["OpenAI:Model"] ?? "gpt-4o";
    }

    public async Task<string> CompleteAsync(string prompt, CancellationToken ct = default)
    {
        var response = await _client.GetChatClient(_model)
            .CompleteChatAsync(prompt, cancellationToken: ct);
        return response.Value.Content[0].Text;
    }
}

// Easily swap to another provider:
// services.AddScoped<IAIAssistant, ClaudeAssistant>();
builder.Services.AddScoped<IAIAssistant, OpenAIAssistant>();
```

```csharp
// AI as a cross-cutting concern: smart error handling via Middleware
public class AIEnhancedErrorMiddleware
{
    private readonly RequestDelegate _next;
    private readonly IAIAssistant _ai;

    public AIEnhancedErrorMiddleware(RequestDelegate next, IAIAssistant ai)
    {
        _next = next;
        _ai = ai;
    }

    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
    {
        try
        {
            await _next(context);
        }
        catch (Exception ex)
        {
            var suggestion = await _ai.CompleteAsync(
                $"Suggest a user-friendly error message for: {ex.GetType().Name}: {ex.Message}");

            context.Response.StatusCode = 500;
            await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new
            {
                Error = "An error occurred",
                Suggestion = suggestion
            });
        }
    }
}
```

### Feature Flags as Dependencies (AI On Demand)

```csharp
public class ProductService
{
    private readonly IFeatureManager _features;
    private readonly IAIAssistant _ai;
    private readonly IProductRepository _repo;

    public ProductService(IFeatureManager features, IAIAssistant ai, IProductRepository repo)
    {
        _features = features;
        _ai = ai;
        _repo = repo;
    }

    public async Task<ProductDescription> GetDescriptionAsync(int id)
    {
        var product = await _repo.GetByIdAsync(id);

        if (await _features.IsEnabledAsync("AIDescriptions"))
        {
            var enhanced = await _ai.CompleteAsync(
                $"Enhance product description: {product!.Description}");
            return new ProductDescription(enhanced);
        }

        return new ProductDescription(product!.Description);
    }
}
```

---

## OpenTelemetry: The Modern Observability Aspect

```csharp
builder.Services.AddOpenTelemetry()
    .WithTracing(tracing => tracing
        .AddAspNetCoreInstrumentation()
        .AddEntityFrameworkCoreInstrumentation()
        .AddHttpClientInstrumentation()
        .AddOtlpExporter(opt => opt.Endpoint = new Uri("http://jaeger:4317")))
    .WithMetrics(metrics => metrics
        .AddAspNetCoreInstrumentation()
        .AddRuntimeInstrumentation()
        .AddPrometheusExporter());

public class OrderService
{
    private static readonly ActivitySource _activity = new("MyApp.OrderService");

    public async Task<Order> CreateOrderAsync(CreateOrderCommand cmd)
    {
        using var span = _activity.StartActivity("CreateOrder");
        span?.SetTag("order.customerId", cmd.CustomerId);

        // business logic...
        return order;
    }
}
```

---

## Comparing AOP Approaches in 2025

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ASP.NET Core Middleware** | Built-in, simple, HTTP-centric | HTTP requests only | HTTP logging, auth, CORS |
| **Decorator Pattern** | Explicit, testable, no magic | More boilerplate | Caching, validation |
| **MediatR Behaviors** | Great for CQRS, flexible | Requires MediatR | Commands/Queries pipeline |
| **Scrutor Decorators** | Convention-based registration | Depends on MS DI | Mass decoration |
| **Castle Windsor Interceptors** | Powerful, AOP without attributes | Complex setup | Legacy + enterprise |
| **PostSharp / Metalama** | Compile-time weaving, fast | Paid, magic in code | Financial systems, auditing |
| **Source Generators** | Compile-time, no runtime overhead | Hard to write | High-throughput systems |

---

## References & Further Reading

### Books

- **Mark Seemann, Steven van Deursen** — *Dependency Injection Principles, Practices, and Patterns* (Manning, 2019)
- **Geoffrey West** — *Scale: The Universal Laws of Growth, Innovation, Sustainability, and the Pace of Life in Organisms, Cities, Economies, and Companies* (Penguin Press, 2017)
- **Martin Fowler** — [Inversion of Control Containers and the Dependency Injection pattern](https://martinfowler.com/articles/injection.html)
- **Robert C. Martin** — *Agile Principles, Patterns, and Practices in C#*

### Tools & Frameworks

- [Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/extensions/dependency-injection)
- [Autofac](https://autofac.org/)
- [Scrutor](https://github.com/khellang/Scrutor) — convention-based decoration
- [MediatR](https://github.com/jbogard/MediatR) — CQRS + pipeline behaviors
- [Metalama](https://www.postsharp.net/metalama) — modern Roslyn-based successor to PostSharp
- [OpenTelemetry .NET](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/languages/dotnet/)
- [Polly](https://github.com/App-vNext/Polly) — resilience & fault tolerance
- [Microsoft.FeatureManagement](https://github.com/microsoft/FeatureManagement-Dotnet)

### AI & LLM Dependencies

- [Semantic Kernel](https://github.com/microsoft/semantic-kernel) — AI orchestration for .NET
- [Microsoft.Extensions.AI](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/introducing-microsoft-extensions-ai-preview/) — abstraction layer over AI providers
- [Azure OpenAI Service](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/)

---

*Mikhail Strutinsky, 2013–2026*
